When I deliver a keynote presentation on the principle of social proof, it's not uncommon for an audience member or two to ask an important question. What do I do if I don't have social proof to point to? What if I have a little known startup company or I have a new product with nothing impressive to talk about in the way of market share or sales numbers or general popularity at this point? What do I do then? I always responded by saying, well, you certainly shouldn't lie about the lack of social proof. Instead, use one of the other principles you might have going for you, such as authority or liking. Scarcity might be a good one. However, recent research indicates that my advice to steer clear of social proof evidence if it's not present is mistaken. Rather than relying only on evidence of existing social proof, a communicator can do at least as well by relying on evidence of future social proof. When people notice a trend, they expect that it will likely continue. In fact, people believe the trends will persist for a wide variety of behaviors, including those undertaken by only a minority of others, such as conserving water, choosing meatless meals, and completing surveys for no payment. Take a look at this graphic. The first bar shows 10% of people performing the desired behavior, let's say it's conserving water in the home, at .1. The next bar shows 20% doing so later at .2 in time, and the next bar shows 30% of the people doing so still later at .3. After seeing these increasing data points, observers predict that the trend will continue until the majority of people eventually perform the desired behavior. So if you only have a minority of people moving in the direction of a new product or model or feature, but you can show growth in the form of a trend, be sure to make that fact a central feature of your messaging, because your audience will take it to be an indicator of future popularity and will want to get in on the ground floor.
